inputs.prompt: Newsletter: The Signal. Voice: Direct, analytical, slightly contrarian. Like a smart colleague who reads everything so you don't have to. Audience: media professionals and journalists. Main story: The rise of AI-generated news summaries is creating a new problem — readers trust the summaries more than the original reporting, and some AI tools are now being cited as sources in other news articles. I want to argue this is an attribution crisis, not a technology problem. Links: 1) Reuters Institute study on AI news consumption. 2) A journalist who discovered her original reporting recycled verbatim by an AI news tool. 3) An op-ed by an editor arguing AI summaries are 'the new wire service.' Word count: ~700 words.
Newsletter: Weekly Dispatch. Voice: Friendly, casual. Topic: The best podcasts I listened to this week. Links: 3 podcast episodes. Word count: 500.

Newsletter: The Brussels Memo. Voice: Informed, measured, occasionally wry. Audience: EU policy professionals and journalists. Main story: The European Commission's new proposal to regulate 'systemic risk' in large AI models has a hidden clause that would require open-source AI projects to register with a new EU agency. Open-source advocates say this effectively ends open AI development in Europe. Links: 1) The Commission's draft proposal text. 2) A letter signed by 200 European AI researchers opposing the clause. 3) An interview with the Commissioner defending the approach. Word count: ~800.
